summary:
The presence of bilious vomiting shortly after birth suggests intestinal obstruction proximal to the ampulla of Vater—most commonly seen in duodenal atresia rather than pyloric stenosis which causes non-bilious emesis later in infancy. Additionally, absence of meconium passage until now may be misleading because while it can occur early in life during certain conditions like Hirschsprung disease, many cases present without passing stool initially even if they have normal colonic function postnatally—and thus cannot rule out other forms such as duodenal atresias given their similar timing patterns. However, crucially important findings include high amniotic fluid levels indicating fetal compromise related directly to poor digestion/gastric retention causing excess AFP release into circulation—a known feature observed specifically within pregnancies affected by duodenal atresia due to reduced absorption capacity affecting overall metabolic balance including amino acid turnover processes involved therein. This finding combined with history of maternal gestational diabetes further strengthens suspicion toward duodenal pathology over alternative diagnoses such as necrotizing enterocolitis—which usually occurs only under specific circumstances involving premature infants exposed to formula feeding environments lacking protective factors found naturally occurring in breast milk—as well as meconium ileus linked more closely with cystic fibrosis pathophysiological mechanisms requiring genetic testing confirmation before definitive attribution could take place here based solely upon available information provided so far.

Final Answer: \boxed{A}

Based on these observations—the combination of immediate bilious vomiting along with elevated AFIs pointing clearly back toward disrupted digestive pathways caused predominantly through anatomical defects particularly those impacting upper small intestine structures—we maintain confidence that choice A represents best explanation among all listed alternatives considering full context presented across various domains ranging from neonatal signs up through antepartum imaging results reflecting underlying physiological disturbances preceding actual symptom manifestation itself.
